name: large gear
shortDescription: A large gear with heavy teeth and a workshop-darkened finish, built for mills, lifts, gate mechanisms, and ambitious machines that prefer strength over silence. Engineers judge it by the bite of its cogs; merchants judge it by how many porters complain when it moves.
flavorText: “Big gears make small hands feel clever.”

name: large tent
shortDescription: A large tent of sturdy canvas, poles, cords, and patched wisdom, suitable for market stalls, field camps, caravan stops, or traveling officials who dislike sleeping like everyone else. It turns bare ground into temporary property with remarkable speed.
flavorText: “A tent is a room that has not decided where it lives.”

name: oil lamp
shortDescription: An oil lamp with a small belly, soot-marked spout, and enough steady light to make ledgers readable after dusk. Inns, watch posts, shrine corners, and late-working shops all depend on such lamps when candles are too proud and darkness too costly.
flavorText: “A little oil can buy one more hour of business.”

name: pedestal
shortDescription: A plain pedestal meant to lift statues, relics, jars, trophies, or expensive mistakes into public view. It does not matter what rests on top; the pedestal’s duty is to make people look up and assume the object deserves it.
flavorText: “Half of importance is height.”

name: pickaxe
shortDescription: A pickaxe with a practical head and a handle polished by hard hands, used for mining seams, breaking stone, and discovering whether a hill is worth the trouble. It is a miner’s argument with the earth, repeated until profit appears.
flavorText: “Mountains rarely open politely.”

name: forked rod
shortDescription: A forked rod cut, dried, and balanced for dowsers, hedge-mages, and anyone who believes wood can hear water better than people can. Buyers ask for a straight branch until they want guidance, then suddenly pay extra for a crooked one.
flavorText: “It points best when no one is watching too closely.”

name: rope
shortDescription: A coil of rope twisted from tough fiber, ready for wagons, wells, pulleys, boats, tents, bundles, and hurried repairs. It is one of those humble goods that seems overpriced only until the moment nothing else will hold.
flavorText: “Civilization is mostly knots pretending to be arrangements.”

name: shovel
shortDescription: A shovel with a broad worn blade and a handle made for repetitive honesty. Farmers, diggers, gravekeepers, road crews, and stubborn homeowners all need one, because dirt keeps arriving in places people would rather it did not.
flavorText: “The ground always negotiates from below.”

name: small gear
shortDescription: A small gear with neat teeth and surprising importance, suited to clocks, locks, counters, clever toys, and delicate workshop devices. It proves that machinery often depends on parts too small for anyone to praise until they fail.
flavorText: “Tiny teeth can stop a giant machine.”

name: square stone
shortDescription: A square stone cut into a simple block, useful for foundations, paving, platforms, steps, and the kind of construction that becomes respectable after enough repetition. It is not decorative, but straight edges have a market all their own.
flavorText: “A square stone is a promise that the wall will behave.”

name: square stone base
shortDescription: A square stone base shaped to support statues, posts, braziers, signs, or whatever else needs to stand with more dignity than its weight deserves. Buyers rarely admire the base, but they blame it first if anything tilts.
flavorText: “The bottom of glory is usually stone.”

name: square cracked stone
shortDescription: A square cracked stone block with useful shape and a visible warning through its face. It still serves for rough paths, patched walls, training yards, or cheap foundations, though honest sellers point out the crack before the buyer hears it later.
flavorText: “Some stones are discounted because they tell the truth too clearly.”

name: sticks
shortDescription: A bundle of sticks gathered for kindling, markers, splints, simple frames, garden rows, children’s mischief, and countless temporary fixes. They are nearly worthless one at a time and strangely useful when tied together by someone practical.
flavorText: “A stick is cheap. A bundle is a plan.”

name: straw training dummy
shortDescription: A straw training dummy tied into a patient humanoid shape for drill yards and nervous apprentices. It absorbs clumsy strikes, bad balance, and overconfident speeches without complaint, which makes it more valuable than many living instructors admit.
flavorText: “The dummy never corrects you. That is both kindness and danger.”

name: sythe
shortDescription: A sythe with a long curved blade and a handle made for sweeping work through grass, grain, or thick weeds. Farmers know its rhythm well, and merchants know the season it sells best: just before everyone remembers the fields are growing faster than hands.
flavorText: “Harvest tools teach patience with a sharp edge.”

name: wooden training dummy
shortDescription: A wooden training dummy with carved arms, a battered trunk, and enough resilience to embarrass beginners. It stands in yards where soldiers, guards, and ambitious youths learn that wood can be a very stern teacher.
flavorText: “Bruised knuckles are cheaper than bruised pride in real company.”

name: wrench
shortDescription: A wrench forged for gripping bolts, nuts, pipe fittings, and obstinate workshop parts that fingers cannot persuade. Millwrights, artificers, wagon repairers, and machine keepers treat it as a key for things that are not supposed to open.
flavorText: “A wrench is diplomacy for metal that will not listen.”

name: nails
shortDescription: A supply of nails, small iron promises that hold roofs, crates, doors, fences, shelves, carts, and half-finished repairs together. They are bought in dull quantities and missed in dramatic moments when the last one rolls under a bench.
flavorText: “Every grand hall contains thousands of tiny agreements.”

name: lockpick
shortDescription: A slender lockpick shaped for delicate work inside stubborn mechanisms. Locksmiths buy them openly, collectors buy them carefully, and suspicious customers insist they only need one because an old chest has forgotten its key.
flavorText: “Some tools are legal until the hand holding them changes.”

name: lock
shortDescription: A metal lock with a solid case and a promise of privacy, commonly fitted to chests, shutters, storerooms, gates, and anything else valuable enough to make people lie. Its worth depends on the buyer believing delay is almost the same as safety.
flavorText: “A lock does not stop greed. It asks greed to make noise.”

name: soap
shortDescription: A bar of soap made from fat, ash, scent, and the daily hope that travel, work, and livestock can be washed away. Inns sell it to guests, healers demand it, and merchants learn that cleanliness becomes luxury on long roads.
flavorText: “Soap is civilization in a slippery square.”

name: scarecrow
shortDescription: A scarecrow of straw, cloth, poles, and vacant menace, built to guard fields from birds and perhaps from neighbors with weak imaginations. Its success depends less on craft than on distance, wind, and a crow’s willingness to be impressed.
flavorText: “The best scarecrow looks almost like someone who might complain.”

name: small sign
shortDescription: A small signboard ready for prices, warnings, directions, stall names, or hurried notes nailed beside a road. It offers only a little space, which is useful when the message must be short enough for passing customers to obey.
flavorText: “Small signs sell best when the arrow is larger than the explanation.”

name: medium sign
shortDescription: A medium sign with enough room for a shop name, service list, guild mark, or properly dramatic warning. Tavern keepers, road wardens, and market sellers like it because it can be noticed without needing to become architecture.
flavorText: “A good sign is a quiet person shouting all day.”

name: large sign
shortDescription: A large sign built for distance, crowds, and owners who want no traveler to claim they did not see it. It belongs above inns, warehouses, toll posts, and festival stalls where being noticed is worth the extra timber.
flavorText: “When letters grow large enough, even pride becomes readable.”

name: billow
shortDescription: A billow of leather, wood, and careful hinges, made to feed breath into forge fires, hearths, and workshop flames. Smiths know it as the quiet lung of hot labor, and apprentices know it as the reason their arms ache before noon.
flavorText: “Fire grows bold when someone else does the breathing.”

name: chisel
shortDescription: A chisel with a keen working edge, used by masons, carvers, carpenters, and anyone who removes small pieces until shape appears. It is less dramatic than a hammer, but much better at leaving intention behind.
flavorText: “A chisel wins by taking away everything that is not needed.”

name: clamp
shortDescription: A clamp made to hold boards, metal, leather, or glued work steady while hands are busy elsewhere. Craftspeople value it because patience is easier when a tool is doing the gripping and no apprentice is being asked to stand still.
flavorText: “The clamp’s virtue is stubborn helpfulness.”

name: cutters
shortDescription: A pair of cutters with strong jaws for wire, thin rod, cord, chain links, and stubborn bits of craftwork that refuse neat separation. They sit near benches where repair becomes redesign in a single squeeze.
flavorText: “Cutters are for when untangling becomes philosophy.”

name: gauge
shortDescription: A gauge marked for measuring thickness, gaps, pressure, or some specialized workshop standard that buyers argue about with great seriousness. It keeps craftsmen honest, machines tuned, and apprentices from guessing with expensive confidence.
flavorText: “A gauge is a small ruler with authority.”

name: grindstone
shortDescription: A grindstone worn smooth at its rim, used to sharpen blades, dress tools, and restore edges dulled by honest work. It earns coin slowly, one turn at a time, until every knife and axe in a village owes it respect.
flavorText: “The stone grows smaller so other tools may stay sharp.”

name: stone worktable
shortDescription: A stone worktable built for heat, pounding, cutting, stains, and all the tasks that would shame a wooden bench. Alchemists, cooks, smiths, and dyers like its refusal to burn, warp, or remember spills too personally.
flavorText: “Some work demands a table that fears nothing ordinary.”

name: spindle
shortDescription: A spindle for drawing fiber into thread with practiced fingers and patient turning. It looks simple until a novice tries to use it, then suddenly becomes proof that cloth begins long before anyone calls it fabric.
flavorText: “Thread is patience made thin.”

name: spindle and wheel
shortDescription: A spindle and wheel set for faster spinning, favored by households and workshops that turn fleece, flax, or cotton into steady thread. It hums through long evenings, converting quiet labor into something merchants can finally count by the bundle.
flavorText: “The wheel makes patience look productive.”

name: loom
shortDescription: A loom with a wooden frame, taut lines, and the orderly promise of cloth. Weavers use it to turn thread into fabric, patterns into income, and long hours into goods fine enough for tailors to criticize professionally.
flavorText: “A loom teaches threads how to become a surface.”

name: tablesaw
shortDescription: A tablesaw set into a sturdy work surface for cutting boards, beams, and panels with more consistency than a tired hand can manage. Carpenters respect it, apprentices fear it, and merchants sell it only to workshops that understand attention.
flavorText: “Measure twice, feed slowly, keep all fingers invested.”

name: horseshoe
shortDescription: A horseshoe shaped to protect hooves over roads, stones, mud, and long caravan miles. Farriers buy them in practical numbers, while teamsters know a missing shoe can delay more profit than a broken promise.
flavorText: “A horse’s luck is often nailed on.”

name: saddle
shortDescription: A saddle of leather, straps, padding, and road-tested shape, made to turn animal strength into travel without turning the rider into cargo. Quality matters most after the first day, when cheap stitching begins to explain itself.
flavorText: “The saddle is where distance negotiates with comfort.”

name: mallet
shortDescription: A mallet with a broad head for striking without ruining the thing being struck. Carpenters, tent crews, coopers, and stone workers use it when force is needed but dents are not part of the design.
flavorText: “A mallet is a hammer that learned manners.”

name: pickaxe
shortDescription: A second pickaxe stock pattern, balanced for quarry hands and prospectors who prefer a narrower bite. It breaks rock, loosens packed ground, and reminds every buyer that mining is mostly hope attached to a handle.
flavorText: “Different pick, same mountain.”

name: hoe
shortDescription: A hoe with a practical blade for turning soil, cutting weeds, and preparing rows before seeds become promises. Farmers buy them before spring with grim certainty, because fields do not care how sharp anyone’s excuses are.
flavorText: “A hoe makes tomorrow’s harvest look like today’s blisters.”

name: scissors
shortDescription: Scissors with matched blades for cloth, thread, paper, hair, herbs, and every small cut that should not be trusted to a knife. Tailors judge them by the sound they make through fabric and the lies they spare from bad measuring.
flavorText: “Good scissors make mistakes cleaner, if not rarer.”

name: tongs
shortDescription: Iron tongs made for gripping hot metal, coals, pots, or troublesome things that hands have wisely refused. Forges, kitchens, and kilns all keep them close because bravery is no substitute for distance from heat.
flavorText: “Tongs are the shape of caution.”

name: working knife
shortDescription: A working knife with a plain handle and a blade meant for rope, leather, food prep, whittling, scraping, and quick repairs. It is not a noble weapon; it is better, because someone useful reaches for it every day.
flavorText: “A working knife earns its edge in small decisions.”

name: cotton
shortDescription: Cotton fiber, soft, pale, and ready for spinning into thread that becomes cloth, bandages, padding, and comfortable garments. It travels lightly compared with its value, provided it stays dry and away from careless sparks.
flavorText: “Soft cargo still demands careful hands.”

name: wool
shortDescription: Wool gathered from shorn fleece, warm, springy, and rich with the promise of blankets, cloaks, socks, felt, and winter profit. It smells of pasture before it smells of dye, but cold customers rarely complain for long.
flavorText: “Wool is summer grass saved for winter backs.”

name: flax fiber
shortDescription: Flax fiber stripped and prepared for spinning into linen, pale and wiry before it becomes smooth cloth. Weavers like it for strength, merchants like it for steady demand, and laundresses like it when buyers can afford the better grade.
flavorText: “Linen begins as a stubborn plant with elegant ambitions.”

name: hemp fiber
shortDescription: Hemp fiber, tough and useful, destined for rope, canvas, sacks, sailcloth, and other goods that must survive weather and rough hands. It is not delicate material, but trade routes rarely run on delicate material alone.
flavorText: “Hemp becomes the things that hold everything else together.”

name: ramie fiber
shortDescription: Ramie fiber with a clean strength and fine textile promise, prized by weavers who want cloth that keeps its shape and takes dye with quiet confidence. It is less common than flax, which gives careful merchants room to make a better margin.
flavorText: “Rare fibers turn plain thread into negotiation.”

name: fur
shortDescription: Prepared fur with a warm pile and a frontier reputation, bought for trims, linings, blankets, winter hats, and status displays that pretend to be practical. Quality depends on softness, season, color, and whether the buyer asks too many questions about origin.
flavorText: “Fur is warmth with a story people may not want finished.”
